sections.heading: Core distinction
sections.content: Authentication answers who the user is; authorization answers what that authenticated user may do. In a logistics platform, a driver, warehouse operator, planner and administrator may all authenticate through the same identity provider but receive different permissions.

sections.heading: OAuth2 and OpenID Connect
sections.content: OAuth2 is an authorization framework. OpenID Connect adds identity on top of OAuth2. A browser or mobile client normally uses Authorization Code with PKCE. The application receives an access token for APIs and optionally an ID token containing identity claims.

sections.heading: Roles and permissions
sections.content: Prefer permission-based checks such as DELIVERY_READ or ROUTE_ASSIGN over broad role checks. Roles can group permissions, but business code should depend on the smallest capability required. Apply authorization at both API boundaries and sensitive service methods.

sections.heading: Interview answer
sections.content: I separate authentication from authorization, use short-lived access tokens, validate issuer, audience, signature and expiry, and enforce least privilege. For high-risk operations I also require audit logging and sometimes step-up authentication.

sections.heading: Security filter chain
sections.content: Spring Security processes requests through a SecurityFilterChain. Configure public endpoints explicitly, require authentication by default, and place the most specific matchers before broader ones.

sections.heading: JWT resource server
sections.content: A Spring Boot API can act as an OAuth2 Resource Server. It validates JWT signatures using the identity provider's JWK set and converts claims or scopes into GrantedAuthority values.

sections.heading: Method security
sections.content: Use @PreAuthorize for sensitive business actions, for example @PreAuthorize("hasAuthority('ROUTE_ASSIGN')"). Method security protects the use case even when it is called from another controller or integration path.

sections.heading: Production guidance
sections.content: Do not disable security globally for convenience. Keep actuator endpoints restricted, avoid logging tokens, use secure cookies for browser sessions, and test both allowed and denied scenarios.

title: Web Security: XSS, CSRF and CORS

sections.heading: XSS
sections.content: Cross-site scripting happens when untrusted content is rendered as executable HTML or JavaScript. Escape output, sanitize rich text, avoid unsafe innerHTML, and use a strict Content Security Policy.

sections.heading: CSRF
sections.content: CSRF abuses a browser's automatic credential submission. It matters mainly for cookie-based sessions. Use Spring Security CSRF protection and SameSite cookies. Pure bearer-token APIs that do not use cookies are generally not vulnerable in the same way.

sections.heading: CORS
sections.content: CORS is a browser access-control mechanism, not authentication. Allow only known origins, methods and headers. Never combine wildcard origins with credentials.

sections.heading: Interview answer
sections.content: I treat XSS, CSRF and CORS as different problems: output encoding for XSS, anti-forgery controls for cookie sessions, and a narrow origin policy for CORS.

sections.heading: Service-to-service authentication
sections.content: Internal services should authenticate each other using workload identity, mTLS or OAuth2 client credentials. Network location alone is not proof of identity.

sections.heading: Resilience and abuse controls
sections.content: Apply rate limits, request-size limits, timeouts and circuit breakers. Validate payloads at the boundary and reject unknown or dangerous fields.

sections.heading: Secrets
sections.content: Store secrets in a secret manager or Kubernetes Secret integrated with encryption and access control. Never commit credentials, private keys or production tokens to source control.

sections.heading: Auditability
sections.content: Log security-relevant business events such as route reassignment, permission changes and manual delivery overrides. Logs should identify actor, action, target, time and outcome without exposing sensitive data.

sections.heading: Data minimization
sections.content: Collect and retain only the personal data required for the operational purpose. Driver location data, customer addresses and phone numbers require clear purpose and restricted access.

sections.heading: Protection
sections.content: Encrypt data in transit with TLS and at rest using platform-managed encryption. Mask sensitive fields in logs and non-production databases.

sections.heading: GDPR principles
sections.content: Key principles include lawfulness, purpose limitation, data minimization, accuracy, storage limitation, integrity, confidentiality and accountability.

sections.heading: Operational design
sections.content: Define retention periods, deletion workflows, access auditing and incident response before production. Privacy requirements must be part of architecture, not an afterthought.
